coredata.dc:title: Boosting lithium–sulfur battery performance using a phosphating nano-sulfur anchored on graphene framework anode

coredata.dc:description: The shuttle effect of the intermediate lithium polysulfide and low sulfur utilization rate are two core issues restricting the further development of lithium–sulfur batteries (LSBs). Herein, a hierarchical sandwich superstructure (abbreviate as P-NS/GQDs/MGO) compose of phosphating nano-sulfur (P-NS) particles disperse and anchor on monolayer graphene oxide (MGO) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the intermediate phase is constructed by hydrothermal method under oxygen-assist atmosphere and apply to the cathodes of LSBs. Notably, the phosphating and the robustly anchored P-NS effectively restricts the dissolution of intermediates in ether-based electrolytes and boosts charge/ion transport, which promotes the redox kinetics and maintains the electrode integrity during electrochemical cycling. Moreover, GQDs can facilitate the formation of specific bonding environments and interfacial interactions, resulting in accelerate heterogeneous nucleation, which effectively prevents P-NS agglomeration and volume change, improves sulfur utilization. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the synergy of strategies relate to phosphating and interfacial interactions lead to high energy density and ultra-long cycle life with specific discharge capacities of 1117 and 838 mAh g−1 at 0.1 and 1.68A g−1, respectively. The capacity decay rate per cycle is 0.041% after 1000 charge–discharge cycles at 1.68A g−1.

head.ce:title: Boosting lithium–sulfur battery performance using a phosphating nano-sulfur anchored on graphene framework anode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science & Ministry-province Jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science & Ministry-province Jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The shuttle effect of the intermediate lithium polysulfide and low sulfur utilization rate are two core issues restricting the further development of lithium–sulfur batteries (LSBs). Herein, a hierarchical sandwich superstructure (abbreviate as P-NS/GQDs/MGO) compose of phosphating nano-sulfur (P-NS) particles disperse and anchor on monolayer graphene oxide (MGO) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the intermediate phase is constructed by hydrothermal method under oxygen-assist atmosphere and apply to the cathodes of LSBs. Notably, the phosphating and the robustly anchored P-NS effectively restricts the dissolution of intermediates in ether-based electrolytes and boosts charge/ion transport, which promotes the redox kinetics and maintains the electrode integrity during electrochemical cycling. Moreover, GQDs can facilitate the formation of specific bonding environments and interfacial interactions, resulting in accelerate heterogeneous nucleation, which effectively prevents P-NS agglomeration and volume change, improves sulfur utilization. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the synergy of strategies relate to phosphating and interfacial interactions lead to high energy density and ultra-long cycle life with specific discharge capacities of 1117 and 838 mAh g−1 at 0.1 and 1.68A g−1, respectively. The capacity decay rate per cycle is 0.041% after 1000 charge–discharge cycles at 1.68A g−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium–sulfur batteries (LSBs) are regarded as a promising successor of next-generation energy storage devices applied to civilian or military fields because of their abundance, low cost, environmental friendliness, and high theoretical capacity (1675 mAh g−1 for sulfur) [1,2]. However, the practical application of LSBs is still greatly hindered by the slow redox kinetics and shuttle effect caused by the dissolution of lithium polysulfides (LiPs) [3–5]. Recently, engineering sulfur in nanoscale and supporting nano-sulfur (NS) particles on carbonaceous conductive stress buffer materials, such as monolayer graphene oxide (MGO), have received increasing attention in tackling these problems owing to the unique nano-size effect of sulfur [6,7]. The nanostructured NS particles makes “dead sulfur” rarely exist inside NS and easier to contact with the transferred Li+/electron, which effectively promotes diffusion kinetics [8–10]. Nonetheless, its inherent insulating properties face challenges in practical applications, especially the more sufficient contact area between NS and electrolyte, leading to more sluggish reaction kinetics and vigorous shuttle effect [11,12]. Recent theoretical simulation results revealed that compositional tuning (phosphating) can regulate the energy gap between bonding and antibonding orbitals, and less electron-pull effect leads to increased energy of bonding state, which accordingly boosts the interfacial redox dynamics [13]. As a result, the electronic conductivity of sulfur is enhanced by phosphating [14,15], which also facilitates the spatial distribution of electroactive sites and makes sulfur reinforce the interactions between atoms/chains and host molecules from surface to bulk [16]. In this sense, utilizing the phosphating strategy is important to promote the conversion of polysulfides during the charge–discharge process and improve the reaction kinetics. However, this strategy has been rarely concerned.

NS with high active surface energy always reveals a poor monodispersity and low physical attachment in composites and thus tends to agglomerate into a bulk, leading to the decreased utilization efficiency of active NS and accelerated capacity fading [9,17,18]. An important but often overlooked fact is that the poor interface compatibility and low physical confinement of NS with MGO lead to conversion-induced stress-driven NS extrusion and free movement, further agglomeration and irreversible detachment from the substrate, reduced cycling stability, and even battery failure and thus present challenging kinetic problems. In detail, the loose attachment and free movement of NS combined force represent the main reasons for the agglomeration and shuttle effect. Hence, the dispersion nature and immobility of individual NS particles within the MGO support, which improves the structural stability and the charge transport of Li+/electrons, have become extremely important in architectural design and pose a formidable technical challenge [19]. To this end, special consideration was given to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with hyperdispersive properties, crystalline sp2‐hybridized core and abundant edge sites as flexible linkers to form the specific bonding environments and interfacial interactions between MGO and NS [20–22], which facilitate the stable dispersion and anchoring of NS and limit agglomeration and exfoliation with accelerated NS nucleation [23]. Therefore, exploiting the dispersive and functional characteristics of GQDs inducing the coordination interaction between the MGO support and isolated NS in electrochemical electrodes is practical to suppress aggregation and maintain structural stability [24], which has not been explored to the best of our knowledge.

Considering the effect of phosphating on redox reactions and GQD-induced dispersion and the confined movement of individual NS on kinetic and rapid capacity issues, a stable cathode composite (P-NS/GQDs/MGO) was designed and synthesized using GQDs to induce uniform dispersion and by anchoring individual phosphating nano-sulfur (P-NS) on MGO through strong chemical bonding. This novel phosphating and interfacial interaction strategy offers several benefits. (1) P-NS can improve the conductivity of sulfur through modulating its electronic structure, and the shuttle effect is eliminated by the resulting ether-based electrolyte-insoluble intermediate; therefore, the rate capability is boosted. (2) The monodispersed nature of P-NS (approximately 80 nm in size) prevents P-NS from being congealed into a bulk, avoiding the stress concentration among NS to realize the agglomeration-free charge storage process and improve the reversible utilization of active materials. (3) P-NS particles were immobilized on MGO through strong chemical bonding via GQDs by forming covalent heterostructures, which ensure the structural integrity upon cycling. Consequently, the as-prepared P-NS/GQDs/MGO exhibited a remarkably high reversible discharge capacity and an outstanding long-term durability for LSBs. P-NS exhibited a narrowed band gap near the Fermi level, and a strong bonding between NS and MGO was evidenced by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, which helps to enhance charge storage, inhibit P-NS agglomeration, and stabilize electrode structures. After prolonged electrochemical testing, the composite exhibited high specific capacity (1117 and 838 mAh g−1 at 0.1 and 1.68A g−1, respectively) and long cycle life (in 1000 cycles at 1.68 A g−1 with a small capacity decay of 0.041%).

Sublimation sulfur, citric acid anhydrous (C6H8O7, CA), and NaOH were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Industrial red phosphorus, L(+)-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), and polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mn = 400 Da) were sourced from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used directly without any purification. MGO aqueous dispersion was ordered from Hangzhou Gaoxian Technology Co., Ltd. and used directly. Deionized water was used in all aqueous solutions used in the experiments.

GQDs were prepared by a “bottom-up” approach using CA as the precursor as described previously [25]. CA (2 g) was added to a 10 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C. CA turned into a yellow solution after heating for 5 min and an orange solution after 35 min. The –OH and –H between CA molecules underwent substitution reaction resulting in incomplete carbonization to generate GQDs. NaOH was used to adjust the pH to 8.0. Then, the mixed solution was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h (dialysate was renewed within 8 h) to remove excess unreacted CA and freeze-dried. The remaining powder was collected to obtain GQDs.

The P-NS/GQDs/MGO composites were prepared by hydrothermal method. The powder with a red phosphorus–sulfur molar ratio of 1:20 was ball-milled for 1 h to create a better contact and facilitate interaction. The uniformly mixed powder was sealed in a quartz tube under a vacuum atmosphere, heated at 300 °C for 12 h, and cooled naturally at room temperature to obtain a PS compound [26]. A mixture of red phosphorus and sulfur can be converted to phosphorus sulfide by heating to 300 °C. Red phosphorus and sulfur form P4S10 during the initial heating process, and P4S10 is converted to P2S5 with free radicals during the continuous heating process. Moreover, when S8 is heated above 250 °C, it is cleaved into short polysulfide chains at the end of the diradical, and then short polysulfide chains are inserted into the P–S–P bonds of P2S5 to form P–S x –P bonds to generate inorganic sulfides [14]. The prepared inorganic compound shown in Fig. S1a (Supporting Information) is in the form of a dark yellow powder. GQDs (40 mg) were to 40 mL of 2 mg/mL MGO aqueous solution, mixed, shaken for 20 min until uniform, added with 0.16 g sodium L-ascorbate, and heated in a constant-temperature water bath for 2 h. During the heating process, the GQDs were attached to the partially reduced MGO flakes through the combination of ester bonds and hydrogen bonds [27], resulting in partially reduced GQDs/MGO dispersions. PS compound (1.6 g), NaOH (4 g), and PEG (3 g, Mn = 400 Da) were added as the dispersants to a flask filled with 100 mL of water and stirred at 90 °C for 2 h under an oxygen atmosphere. The partially reduced GQDs/MGO dispersion was added to the flask and further stirred for 6 h. The prepared composites were vacuum filtered, centrifuged three times in deionized water, and freeze-dried to obtain P-NS/GQDs/MGO. Using a similar method, a fully reduced composite (r-GQDs/MGO) was obtained by continuing to heat the partially reduced GQDs/MGO dispersion in a water bath for 4 h, followed by the preparation of reduced monolayer oxidation without the addition of GQDs. Then, NS/MGO was prepared without the addition of red phosphorus and GQDs, and P-NS/MGO was prepared without GQDs.

The morphologies and structures of the samples were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 20 TWIN) with Cu-Ka as the radiation source. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Japan MiniFlex-600) was used to analyze the crystal structure of the material at the scanning speed of 5°/min, the cavity voltage of 40kV, and the tube current of 30 mA. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS; S-00123, USA) was used to determine the elemental composition and distribution state of the as-prepared materials. The valence electron distribution and bonding mode of each element were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250XI). The Raman spectrum at 532 nm was measured using a Thermo Fisher-DXR Raman spectrometer. The active substance contents of the prepared materials were determined by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) from room temperature to 600 °C at the heating rate of 10 °C/min. The specific surface area and mesopore size distribution of the prepared samples were determined by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda adsorption methods, respectively.

The active material, super phosphorus, and polyvinylidene fluoride were mixed and ground in a mass ratio of 80:10:10, and n-methylpyrrolidone was added during the grinding process. The ground oily slurry was spread evenly on aluminum foil. The aluminum foil coated with the slurry was vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 12 h and cut into electrode sheets with a diameter of 16 mm, and then water and oxygen contents in the glove box were controlled below 0.1 ppm to assemble a coin cell (CR2025). The prepared electrode sheets were the positive electrode, the Celgard 2400 polypropylene microporous membrane was used as the separator, and the lithium metal sheet was used as the negative electrode. The LS-002 electrolyte was used, the main component was 1,3-dioxolane/ethylene glycol dimethyl ether (1:1 v/v), and 1 wt% LiNO3 additive was added. A positive electrode case, a positive electrode sheet, an electrolyte, a separator, a lithium metal sheet, a foamed nickel, and a negative electrode case were assembled from top to bottom. Shanghai Chenhua Chi750e electrochemical workstation and battery testing system was used to test the electrochemical performance of the battery 24 h after assembly.

All calculations were carried out within the framework of DFT in the Vienna ab initio simulation package using the projection-enhanced plane wave method [28]. Use the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional combined with the generalized gradient approximation as the exchange correlation potential [29]. Long-range van der Waals interactions were described using the DFT-D3 method [30]. The cutoff energy of the plane wave was set to 420 eV. Brillouin zone integration is performed at the gamma point. In the iterative solution of the Kohn-Sham equation, the convergence energy threshold is 10−5 eV. After equilibrium geometry optimization, the residual force of all atoms is reduced to less than 0.05 eV/Å.

The flow chart of P-NS/GQDs/MGO preparation is shown in Fig. 1 . First, the industrial red phosphorus is uniformly mixed with sulfur, the phosphorus atoms are inserted into the opened sulfur atom chain by heating, and the phosphating is achieved. Second, in order to realize the dispersion and immobilization of NS, the GQDs, which act as interfacial connection mediators between P-NS and MGO, were self-assembled with MGO solutions by hydrothermal under strong stirring, so that the GQDs were firmly combined by ester bonds and hydrogen bonds to form a “dot-sheet” structure (GQDs/MGO). To this end, GQDs/MGO was used as a support to synthesize the P-NS/GQDs/MGO composite, which favors the formation of chemical bonding between P-NS and MGO. Then, the PS compound was mixed with the GQDs/MGO aqueous solution in an alkaline environment under an oxygen atmosphere for self-assembly, inorganic sulfides are reduced to divalent polysulfide ions (Sx 2−) in an alkaline environment, and are gradually oxidized to form P-NS in an oxygen atmosphere [18,19]. Subsequently, P-NS covalently bind with GQDs, tightly anchoring P-NS to MGO while maintaining the sufficient spacing between P-NS particles to avoid agglomeration. A hierarchical sandwich superstructure of anchoring P-NS on MGO was fabricated and used as the LSBs cathode. This novel strategy offers the agglomeration free feature for nanoparticles, which is therefore different from the previously reported carbon wrapped or supported nanoparticles.

The morphologies and structures of the prepared samples were investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. S1b−d (Supporting Information), the r-GQDs/MGO composites comprised wrinkled micropieces and had no serious stacking phenomenon. As shown in Figs. S2a and b, graphene was heavily stacked in r-MGO without GQDs, indicating that GQDs hinder MGO agglomeration. GQDs were directly exposed on the MGO with good dispersion (Fig. 2 a). In the high-magnitude TEM image shown in Fig. S3a, the as-prepared GQDs are close to spherical in morphology. Its size distribution histogram (Fig. S3b) shows that the size of GQDs is mostly concentrated around 5 nm. A closer TEM image (Fig. 2b inset) shows that the interplanar spacing was 0.34 nm (fast Fourier transform mode is shown in Fig. S4) with the well-crystallized structure, which can be assigned to the (002) lattice spacing of GQDs. These lattice fringes indicate an abundant interface between GQDs and MGO. The uniform anchoring of GQDs is crucial in minimizing aggregation and regulating interfacial interaction. The SEM images in Fig. 2c–f clearly show that a large number of P-NS in the obtained layered structure attached to the MGO surface. The EDS analysis of phosphorus in the inset of Fig. 2d indicated that phosphorus and sulfur were combined, and NS was phosphorylated. The P-NS in Fig. 2f are well spaced, relatively independent, and in close contact with the MGO, which contribute to structural stability. Notably, the pristine P-NS aggregated into bulk sulfur without GQDs as the dispersant (Fig. S5), which greatly weakened the utilization of sulfur. The TEM images of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO composites (Fig. 2g and h) exhibit a dispersed P-NS morphology with a size of 70–90 nm without obvious aggregation. This outcome is because the multiple functional groups on the GQDs provide sufficient nucleation sites for sulfur growth, anchor P-NS, and limit the further aggregation of P-NS. The EDS elemental map shows the uniform distributions of C, P, and S elements (Fig. 2i). The elemental signals of sulfur, carbon, and phosphorus can be clearly detected with mass percentages of ∼67.58, ∼21.62, and ∼3.67 wt%, respectively, and the mass percentages of oxygen and nitrogen were ∼7.13 wt% as shown in the pie chart inserted in Fig. S6. The results indicate that GQDs induced the anchoring of P-NS on the MGO surface, and the large number of active sites provided by GQDs enabled the strong electronic interactions, strengthened the interfacial coupling in the structure, and thus endowed P-NS with good dispersibility and structural stability, which play a key role in the electrochemical performance of LSBs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to analyze the phase structure and crystal structure of the prepared materials, and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to quantify the active species in the materials [31,32]. The XRD patterns of S, MGO, NS/MGO, P-NS/MGO, and P-NS/GQDs/MGO are shown in Fig. 3 a. MGO has a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.1°. The diffraction peaks are close to the characteristic peaks of graphite, indicating that MGO was well reduced. SN/MGO has a broad diffraction peak at 24°, which is shifted to the left relative to MGO, and has no obvious diffraction peak compared with S, indicating that NS exists in an amorphous form [33]. The diffraction peak width of P-NS/MGO increases due to nanoscale effects and crystal structure defects caused by phosphating [14,34]. From the TGA results shown in Fig. 3b, the three composites lost weight before 100 °C under nitrogen atmosphere because of the evaporation of moisture on the surface and inside of the material; their weights sharply declined between 200 and 350 °C. The weight loss rates of NS/MGO, P-NS/MGO, and P-NS/GQDs/MGO were 66.8%, 71.3%, and 79.8%, respectively. The results showed that the active sites of P-NS/GQDs/MGO increased by the addition of GQDs, and more sulfur can be loaded. In comparison, NS/MGO and P-NS/MGO had a decrease in loading because of particle agglomeration.

The composites were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 3c to further study the differences in conductive MGO support fine structure. The Raman spectrum shows two main peaks concentrated around 1362 (D-band) and 1618 cm−1 (G-band), corresponding to a large number of structural defects in carbon atoms and the vibration of sp2 carbon atoms in 2D hexagonal crystals, respectively [35]. Notably, the intensity ratio of the D and G bands (I D/I G = 1.38) of NS/MGO was slightly higher than that of MGO (1.34). This result indicated that the MGO nanosheets tend to aggregate during thermal reduction because of the strong π-stacking, which reduced the specific surface area of the electrocatalyst and the number of exposed active sites. Additionally, the insertion of NS into the MGO framework created new defects on the edge carbon, and the successful recombination of NS with MGO further improved the disorder of the material. The I D/I G of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO sample was 1.44. This result indicates the successful dispersion of P-NS on MGO and that the doping of phosphorus and the introduction of quantum dots further exerted the edge effect of MGO. The 2D and 2G peaks are manifested around 2704 and 2953 cm−1, respectively. Previous studies have shown that the presence of these two peaks suggests that the stacking or aggregation of MGO layers is reduced, and the transport network of ions and electrons is more efficient, which is beneficial to achieve fast kinetic reactions.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was performed on MGO, NS/MGO, and P-NS/GQDs/MGO to further analyze the insertion of phosphorus and sulfur into the composites (Fig. 3d). The characteristic absorption peaks of graphene oxide are O–H (3000–3500 cm−1), CO (∼1726 cm−1), and CC (1539 cm−1) [36]. The peaks at 527, 1136, and 1273 cm−1 for S/MGO and P-NS/GQDs/MGO are related to S–S stretching, C–O–C stretching (epoxy groups), and C–C deformation, respectively [37]. The peaks at ∼675 and ∼947 cm−1 are related to the stretching of C–S in the sulfur ring and the stretching vibration of S–S, respectively, and the peak at 675 cm−1 of P-NS/GQDs/MGO slightly shifted to the right, which may be due to the phosphating effects on S–S. In addition, the peaks at 1398 cm−1 are characteristic absorption peaks of sulfur, and the C–S stretching vibration has a strong absorption peak at 1357 cm−1, indicating that sulfur is strongly anchored to MGO through C–S [38,39]. After the formation of P-NS/GQDs/MGO, characteristic peaks corresponding to S–P–S stretching vibrations (640 cm−1), as well as the vibrational absorption peaks of PO (1749 cm−1), S–O (1044 cm−1), and P–O–C (987 cm−1), were obtained [38,40,41], which may be formed by the substitution reaction of phosphorus with oxygen-containing functional groups on graphene oxide. P–O–C bonds can strongly bind phosphorus to the carbon matrix, which prevents P-NS from disconnecting from the conductive network during electrochemical cycling and therefore prevents the shuttle effect [41]. The phosphorus atom is regulated by the P-band, and the energy gap between bonding and antibonding orbitals is reduced. Addditionally, the weakening of the electron-pull effect leads to an increase in the energy of the bonding state, which effectively enhances the interfacial interaction. Therefore, the strong chemical interaction between NS and graphene matrix by phosphating can boost structural stability and accelerate electron transfer.

The pore structures and specific surface areas of r-GQDs/MGO and P-NS/GQDs/MGO were examined by N2 adsorption/desorption (Fig. 3e and f). The N2 sorption isotherm exhibits a type Ⅲ pattern. The apparent hysteresis and continuous N2 uptake at high relative pressures (0.9–1.0) reveal the presence of macropores, which is in good agreement with the pore size distribution results (Fig. 3f). The BET specific surface area and total pore volume of r-GQDs/MGO were 254.14 m2 g−1 and 1.88 cm3 g−1, respectively. After loading P-NS, the P-NS/GQDs/MGO still maintain a large specific surface area of 78.47 m2 g−1 and a total pore volume of 0.56 cm3 g−1. The result is attributed to the fact that MGO nanosheets provide a deposition substrate with a large surface area for the nucleation of P-NSs and effectively prevent their agglomeration. Experiments showed that a larger surface area can provide additional electroactive sites for electrochemical reactions, and appropriate mesopores are beneficial for the penetration of electrolytes.

The surface chemical properties of P-NS/GQDs/MGO and r-MGO were further analyzed by XPS. The XPS full-spectrum scans of the samples are shown in Fig. 4 a and confirm the successful recombination of P-NS with the conductive carbon substrate. The C1s spectra of the as-prepared MGO and P-NS/GQDs/MGO (Fig. 4b and d) show that the peaks at 284.8 and 285.6 eV correspond to the sp3 hybridized carbon and C–S bonds, respectively. Moreover, among the C1s test peaks of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO material (Fig. 4d), the peak is the highest at ∼286 eV, indicating that the reduction of the hydroxyl group is replaced by the C–S bond, and the P-NS forms a covalent bond with MGO [42,43]. The peak at 286.2 eV is the CO bond, and the peak corresponding to the carboxyl group is at 288.8 eV [44,45]. After P-NS intercalation, no obvious changes were observed in the two peaks corresponding to C–OH and C–C, indicating a synergistic effect between the carbon-containing groups of the conductive substrate and GQDs. Thus, the chemical bonding of P-NS to graphene is strongly demonstrated. The interfacial linking of GQDs/MGO promoted the bonding between P-NS and graphene and improved the electrochemical stability. Notably, the strong peak at 283.4 eV is attributed to sp2 hybrid carbon, which may be caused by the dispersant ethylenediamine during the preparation. The O1s spectrum in Fig. 4c can be deconvoluted at 530.4, 531.7, 532.8, and 535 eV, which correspond to C–O, C–OH, P–O, and C–O–C bonds, respectively, indicating that P-NS is complexed with MGO and dehydration and oxidation reactions occurred during the process [46]. The P2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 4e) was fitted to two peaks at 132.3 and 133.1 eV, corresponding to the P–S bond. Furthermore, the fitted peak found at 134 eV can be assigned to phosphate. The results confirmed the molecular structure of the PS compound, which is consistent with the previous study by Li et al. [26]. Moreover, the S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 4f) was separated into five peaks consisting of P–S bond (161.7 eV), C–S bond (163.2 eV), S[0] (164.2 eV), sulfur oxide group (167.2 eV), and S–O (168.5 eV) [42,47]. In O2, the surface of P-NS is oxidized, and S x
2− is oxidized resulting in the existence of SO3 2− ions [48]. The contents of highly oxidized sulfur ions (SO3 2−), S–O, S[0], S–S, and P–S were 48.65%, 20.1%, 4.5%, 8.2%, and 18.72%, respectively.

The electrochemical performance is critical to the performance of the battery and is directly determined by the transport rate of electrons and ions inside the battery. A CR2025 coin cell was assembled and evaluated with metallic lithium as the anode and NS/MGO, P-NS/MGO, and P-NS/GQDs/MGO as the cathodes (the active material loading was about 2 mg cm−2, the electrolyte/active material ratio is 10 μL mg−1) to effectively evaluate the electrochemical properties of the prepared materials. The first three CV curves of the P–NS/GQDs/MGO, P-NS/MGO, and NS/MGO cathodes were obtained in 1.5–2.8 V at 0.1 mV s−1 (Fig. 5a and b and S7). The result was consistent with the typical redox peaks of LSBs. Two redox folds appearred at 2.0 and 2.3 V. The higher potential is attributed to the first lithiation process to form polysulfide phosphide/polysulfide (Li3PS4 −/LiPs), and the lower potential corresponds to the further reduction to Li3-x PS4/Li2S2/Li2S. Wrinkles appearred at 2.4 V during anodic cycling, which are ascribed to the oxidation of the precipitated Li2S back to PS/S8. The electrochemical mechanism reaction formula is as follows [14,49]:
(1)

The anodic peak appearred at ∼2.4 V as a smaller oxidation peak, which may be caused by the electrolysis process. The smallest peak separation of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode after continuous cycling indicated its excellent reversibility [50]. In NS/MGO, NS agglomerated to form bulk sulfur because of the absence of phosphating and the anchoring effect of GQDs, resulting in the largest deviation of the cycling curve.

The CV curves of P-NS/MGO, P-NS/GQDs/MGO electrodes at different scan rates were measured to demonstrate that different electrodes improve the diffusion kinetics of Li+ (Fig. 5 c and d), compared with P-NS/MGO, P-NS/GQDs/MGO had narrower CV peaks and minimal peak separation. In addition, analyzing the first charge-discharge CV curves of the three materials (Fig. S8), it can be concluded that compared with P-NS/MGO (0.424 V) and NS/MGO (0.32 V) cathodes, the difference in redox peaks of P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathodes is only 0.398 V, and the peak current is high. The results show that the P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode has faster polysulfide conversion during charge and discharge, which was induced by the abundant edge active sites on the surface of GQDs so that P-NS particles can be tightly attached to the MGO surface [51]. This condition therefore reduced the interface contact resistance between P-NS and MGO and promoted electron transport. Moroever, GQDs prevented the MGO sheets from stacking to form a stratified structure and also helped promote the diffusion of Li+. The Li+ diffusion coefficient (D Li +) can be obtained according to the Randles–Sevick equation [52]:
(3)
S l o p e = 269000 ×
n 1.5
× A ×
D 0.5
× C
where n is the number of electrons, A is the electrode area, and C is the Li+ concentration. The relationships between the peak current and the square root of the scan rate for the P-NS/GQDs/MGO and P-NS/MGO samples are shown in Fig. 5e and f, and the fitting slopes were calculated. The results showed that the slope of the fitting line of P-NS/GQDs/MGO was higher than that of P-NS/MGO, indicating that the interfacial interaction between GQD-linked P-NS and MGO support could induce charge redistribution, which also plays an important role in modifying the electronic structure of active sites and enhancing ion diffusion kinetics.

The cycling performances of P-NS/GQDs/MGO, P-NS/MGO, and NS/MGO as cathodes at the constant current of 0.1 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 6 a. NS/MGO (912 mAh g−1) and NS/MGO (882 mAh g−1) exhibited relatively high initial specific capacities of 1117 mA h g−1. After 200 cycles, the retention rate of P-NS/GQDs/MGO was as high as 81.7%, and the stale Coulombic efficiency (CE) exceeded 97%, indicating that P-NS/GQDs/MGO limited the dissolution of intermediates and improved NS utilization. The excellent electrochemical performance can be attributed to the monodispersed nature of P-NS, in which no intermediate contact occurs between P-NS and therefore avoids the stress concentration between NSs and ensures the structural integrity during cycling. The strong coupling and immobilization of P-NS chemical bonds on MGO buffer tanks to form covalent heterostructures to realize the agglomeration-free charge storage process. By contrast, the poor electrochemical performances of P-NS/MGO and NS/MGO may be due to the lack of edge defects and structural damage, respectively. Fig. S9 shows the constant current charge–discharge curves of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode at 0.1 A g−1 for different cycles, and two typical discharge voltage plateaus were observed, which were consistent with the CV curves. The voltage plateau remained stable after 200 cycles, indicating that the active material was efficiently utilized.

In Fig. 6b, the discharge specific capacity of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode was evaluated by varying the current density from 0.1 to 3.36 A g−1. The cathode exhibited excellent rate performances of 1110, 1034, 924, 873, 798, and 669 mAh g−1. Obviously, the P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode still maintained a capacity of 1052 mAh g−1 when the current rate returned to 0.1 A g−1, indicating that the redox kinetics of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode was accelerated. By contrast, the other cathodes offered lower capacity at the same current rate. The charge–discharge curves of P-NS/GQDs/MGO at the cathodic current densities of 0.1–3.36 A g−1 (Fig. 6c) showed two distinct discharge plateaus even at high rates compared with NS/MGO, and the P-NS/MGO cathodes exhibited potential hysteresis and sharp capacity drop (Figs. S10a and b). These outcomes can be attributed to the introduction of phosphating and GQDs, which greatly enhanced the intermediate anchoring ability and switching.

Electrochemical Nyquist plots before and after cycling were measured to study the electrochemical kinetics of different cathodes. The straight line in the low-frequency region corresponds to the Warburg diffusion process, and the diameter of the semicircle in the high frequency region corresponds to the charge transfer resistance (R ct) generated by the contact between the electrode and electrolyte interface [53]. Compared with P-NS/MGO (R ct = 67) and NS/MGO (R ct = 82), the battery of P-NS/GQDs/MGO had a smaller R ct of 43 (Fig. 6d). In addition, a comparison of the impedance maps of SN/MGO and P-NS/MGO confirmed that the electrical conductivity of elemental sulfur can be enhanced by the formation of covalent bonds through the chemical interaction of phosphorus and sulfur. The chemical bond bridge between P-NS and GQDs enables high electron transfer rate and fast redox kinetics [54]. The resistance of all electrodes dropped considerably after 100 cycles, which can be attributed to the chemical activation and redistribution process of active species (Fig. 6e). The result indicates that the activated active material and the weak migration of LiPs contribute to the diffusion of non-conductive active material and good electrolyte infiltration, and all electrodes have similar high Li+/e− conductivity.

The long-term cycling performances of P-NS/GQDs/MGO, P-NS/MGO, and NS/MGO cathodes at 1.68 A g−1 are shown in Fig. 6f. The P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode exhibited an initial discharge capacity of 838 mAh g−1 and maintained a high capacity of 428 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles, which corresponded to a low capacity decay rate of 0.041% per cycle and a high CE of 94.2%. By contrast, the P-NS/MGO and NS/MGO cathodes had initial capacities of 745 and 679 mAh g−1, respectively, and capacity fading per cycle of 0.059% and 0.080%, respectively. The P-NS/GQDs/MGO cathode exhibited the best cycling stability among these electrodes. This outcome is attributed to the phosphating of NS and the important roles of GQDs in interfacial structure regulation, including stable chemical binding, fast electron/ion transport, and stable interfacial structure with good structural stability and rate performance.

DFT calculation was employed to further understand the effect of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO heterointerface on the P-NS conversion kinetics from the atomic point of view. First, a DFT model was established based on the above features (Fig. 7 a top view and 7b side view). Then, the electronic structure and electronic conductivity of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO hybrid were evaluated. The density of states shown in Fig. 7c shows that the band gap between the valence and conduction bands of the PS compounds formed after phosphating was almost zero, indicating a high electronic conductivity. The result proves that phosphating is beneficial to solid polysulfide nucleation/oxidation. After recombination with the MGO support, the band gap near the Fermi level completely disappearred, and the electronic conductivity wasgreatly improved to accelerate the redox conversion of polysulfides and phosphides [55]. In addition, differential charge density map (Fig. 7d), in the heterointerface constructed by GQDs, the overlapping phenomenon of charge accumulation (yellow) and charge depletion (cyan) between S, O, and C atoms is more obvious, confirming the formation of S–O–C bonds, which effectively inhibit the Shuttle effect, contributing to electrode structure stability [24]. The schematic diagram of the conversion reaction mechanism of P-NS during the charge–discharge process is shown in Fig. 7e. The experimental results and theoretical calculations showed that the GQD-induced self-assembly of P-NS can effectively enhance the inherent hysteretic kinetics and robust structure.

In conclusion, this study used GQDs as the novel structure directing agents to design a stable composite (P-NS/GQDs/MGO) as the cathode for advanced LSBs. The results showed that phosphating remarkably improved the intrinsic conductivity of P-NS, enhanced the binding energy of P-NS and MGO, and generated the insoluble intermediate, Li3PS4, which limits the shuttle effect. In addition, the energy storage active sites of the highly dispersed GQDs were effectively utilized to induce the uniform distribution and strong chemical anchoring of P-NS on the surface of the carbon matrix. The synergistic effect of the two compounds effectively inhibited the shuttle effect and substantially improved the utilization of the active material. The novel composite, P-NS/GQDs/MGO, exhibited good cycling stability over 1000 cycles, a low capacity decay rate of 0.041% per cycle at 1.68 A g−1, and a capacity of 838 mAh g−1 with excellent specific capacity. The rational design of the P-NS/GQDs/MGO nanostructure is expected to provide stable electrode integrity and fast electron or ion transport in LSBs applications, which may be extended to the design of other novel electrochemical energy storage materials and provide a new idea for the practical application of high-performance electrode materials.
